content.text: Answer FOUR questions. Write in a neat and logical fashion, giving complete reasons for all steps.

problem.text:
Let \((a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}\) be a real sequence and for \(n \geq 1\) define
\[
\alpha_n=(a_1+\cdots+a_n)/n.
\]
Prove that
\[
\limsup_{n\to\infty}\alpha_n\leq\limsup_{n\to\infty}a_n
\]
and show by example that the inequality can be strict.

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be metric spaces; give \(X\times Y\) the metric defined by
\[
d((x,y),(a,b))=d_X(x,a)+d_Y(y,b).
\]

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: If \(X\) and \(Y\) are compact, is \(X\times Y\) compact? Proof or counterexample.

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: If \(X\) and \(Y\) are complete, is \(X\times Y\) complete? Proof or counterexample.

problem.text: Let \(f:X\to X\) be a continuous map from a metric space to itself; assume that \(f\) has no fixed points. Prove that if \(X\) is compact then there exists \(\varepsilon>0\) such that \(d(x,f(x))\geq\varepsilon\) for each \(x\in X\).

For a map \(f:X\to Y\) between metric spaces, consider the statements: Of the implications (a) \(\Rightarrow\) (b) and (b) \(\Rightarrow\) (a), one is false and the other true; give a proof of the true and a counterexample for the false.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: \(f\) maps Cauchy sequences to Cauchy sequences.

problem.text: Let \(f:(0,\infty)\to\mathbb{R}\) be differentiable and let \(b\) be a real number. Prove that if \(f'(t)\to b\) as \(t\to\infty\) then \(f(t)/t\to b\) as \(t\to\infty\).
